 

![](data:image/png;base64,)![](data:image/jpeg;base64,)END OF ITERVENTION PERIOD REPORT 

NAME:  

AGE:     

     PROGRAM LEAD: 

REPORT EFFECTIVE DATE: 

__SCORING:__

Still Working On \(SW\) 0%\-25% Emerging \(E\) 25%\-50% Making Progress \(MP\) 50%\-75% Mastered \(M\) 75%\-100%\. 

Success is measured by child’s ability to perform action at least 70% of the time over three consecutive sessions and repeatable over a two\-week period across at least two different settings and persons\.

SCORING

ITEMS/WEEK

SW

E

MP

M

__PROGRESS SUMMARY__

__Sensory\-Motor Integration Domain__

__Baseline skills: She demonstrates improving adaptation to sensory inputs__

Participate in the sensory protocol, including brain gym exercises, joint compression and sensory brushing\.

__*Sensory integration describes the brain processes which allow for information/stimuli received from the senses to be organized and appropriately responded to\. It helps increase an individual’s threshold for tolerating environments, make transitions less disturbing and promote positive behaviours\.*__

She will start and complete motor planning of different movements over a 10\-meter distance by holding onto an item from one end to the other over 10 repetitions, at every given opportunity

Through the just concluded Intervention Period \(IP\), Child participated in both routine sensory activities, and specific tasks that addressed identified sensory adaptation and self\-regulation objectives\.  

She consistently took part in a multi\-sensory program that included therapeutic brushing, joint compression, massage, and various tactile, vestibular, and auditory experiences\. While her tolerance for the varying stimuli presented improved over the course of the IP, Child required prompt to successfully maintain the attention and engagement needed for these sensory activities, which lasted an average of 40 minutes each occasion\. Neutral warmth through blanket wrap is an activity she struggled with at the beginning of the IP, but which her tolerance gradually increased as the IP progressed\. Specifically, we observed that rather than cry or whine for the entire duration of the process, as we progressed into the IP, she would only make a fuss the first seven minutes of the task, displaying more cooperation till the end of an activity that sometimes lasted 15 minutes\. Neutral warmth is necessary for improved calmness, attention span and overall self\-regulation\. 

Child successfully participated in simple motor planning activity by walking from one end of the room to the other over a 10\-meter distance, to pick and drop items into containers over 15 repetitions\. On some occasions, she would do this across two cycles\. Moreover, prompt is required to complete motor planning tasks that include walking on a straight line and stepping over placed obstacles in her path\. When demand however, requires that she maintains a straight line within a designated pathway, Child was often seen walking outside of the path\. She required moderate level redirection to ensure she stayed within the space allocated\.  

Another aspect of sensory processing worked on is auditory\. We introduced different sound textures and pitch at graded intervals to improve her awareness and adaptation to them\. Child tolerated sound played through the music box with the volume carefully graded\. Her increasing ability to comply and continue her engagement indicate improvement in auditory adaptation\. Similarly, Child demonstrated consistent response to her name when called, by occasionally looking in the direction of caller, and responding to ‘come’ motioned to\. This has been observed in both isolated and structured settings\. We have observed this appropriate response at least 60% of the time\. Response in unstructured setting such as outdoor group play is emerging\. 

She will walk on a straight line from one end to another over a pathway of 70 cm width in three out of five attempts\.

She will be able to discriminate among different tastes \(sweet, sour and bitter\) evident by display of appropriate facial expression not more than 5 seconds after exposure, at every given opportunity\.

She will respond to sound no further than 5 metres from her by turning his face in the direction of source, or momentarily pause ongoing activity at every given opportunity\.

She will respond to her name being called by turning his face in the direction of caller, or momentarily pause ongoing activity at every given opportunity in a structured setting\. 

She will respond to her name being called by turning his face in the direction of caller, or momentarily pause ongoing activity in three out of five attempts in an unstructured setting\. 

__Oral Motor Stimulation/Exercise__

__BASELINE SKILL: She occasionally echoes sounds and whole words when prompted__

Participate in jaw massage and other exercises of the oral cavity\.

__*These exercises are crucial to help strengthen the muscles of the oral cavity necessary for feeding and production of speech sounds\. *__

To strengthen her jaw and mouth muscles for speech production and proper articulation, hand massages around her jaw and mouth, along with exercises to encourage different lip and tongue movements were implemented\. Child enjoyed blowing exercises especially when provided with bubbles\. While she successfully blew bubbles some of the time, her ability to produce sound from whistle is emerging\. Furthermore, she has not been able to disperse pieces of paper over a 2\-meter distance\. We will continue to model this to her, and look forward to an improved performance\.  

Child also participated in tongue play aimed at tongue elevation and lateralization \(side\-to\-side\), which is critical for speech clarity and voice pitch\. She has not begun to respond to modeling to move protrude tongue forward, up and sideways\. 

She will move tongue in different direction: forward and sideways, following modeling or in search of sweetness \(honey\) at every given opportunity\.

She will attempt to blow bubbles, water through straw and light items such as powder and paper strips in three out of five attempts to release bubbles and disperse items over a 2\-meter distance in three out of five attempts\.

__Motor Imitation__

__Baseline skills: She has begun to imitate simple actions such as hand clapping, turning around, etc\.__

Imitate 10 motor movements \(with four involving objects\) with minimal prompt: clap, shake a rattle, tap a table, sit and stand up, at every given opportunity\.

__*Motor imitation skill is the ability to mimic or replicate another person’s physical movements, actions or gestures\. Its importance stretches across improving eye\-hand coordination, academic and plays skills\.*__

Child’s motor imitation skill is an area of emerging strength\. She was exposed to imitating single actions, with or without objects, and involving both fine and gross motor components\. We aimed to look at not just her ability to carry out the actions, but on how quickly and accurately she was able to process a visual cue and map it to her own motor behaviours\. Child’s performance in this regard is emerging\. She was able to imitate all actions modelled to her with 60% response rate\. We continue to work on the latency period, as she often required repeated modeling of actions before she carried out the movements\. 

__Listener Responding__

__BASELINE SKILL: She occasionally responds to her name when called, momentarily pausing ongoing activity__

Emit 5 motor actions on command: clap, shake a rattle, tap a table, sit and stand up, at every given opportunity\.  

__*This is the ability to respond to another’s verbal language\. It allows for a child to identify what an item, object, or an activity is, this occurs when the speaker names an item and the listener is able to identify it\. *__

Similar to fluency test on motor imitation, we worked on rapid response to request for motor actions within a 30\-second timed period\. As noted in her response on motor imitation, Child required prompt to sustain attention necessary to carry out familiar actions within the allocated time\. Interfering behaviours include seeking\-movement and minimal tantrum that impact both the quality and quantity of responses within the time frame\. 

We have been impressed with Child’s increasing vocabulary bank, in relation to knowledge of concepts especially when she labels items randomly\. Nevertheless, we have observed her ability to label objects to be limited to categories such as animals, shapes, vegetables, colours, and fruits\. We continue to reinforce her vocabulary by expanding on the number of words she is exposed to\. We have included categories such as actions: sit, stand, clap, dance; occupations: doctor, teacher, lawyer, policeman; locations: on, in, up, down, etc\. Her comprehension of these newer concepts is emerging\. She required physical prompt to respond to request to ‘give me…’ or ‘touch…’\. Although, she is able to randomly label some items\- when requested to, her response has been inconsistent\. She was able to correctly interpret ‘up’ and ‘down’ when manipulating an object, or when on a play equipment such as a slide system was presented, when provided moderate physical prompt\. 

When presented with multiple items in a messy array of about 4 flashcards, Child is able to pick out an item called out to her, demonstrating not just comprehension of words and knowledge of the concept but also emerging attention span and attention\-to\-detail\. These skills are also transferred into a more natural environment as evident in including these demands in classroom routines, to ensure generalisation of concepts\. 

Respond to her name when called by looking in the direction of caller or momentarily pause ongoing activity at every given opportunity in a structured setting\. 

Respond to verbal reprimands: “no”, and “stop”, an average of 2 times per day\.

She will select, point to, or differentially look at total of at least 25 objects, pictures, people, or body parts when named by an adult\.

She will identify at least 25 items in a messy array of at least 4\. 

__Visual Perception/ Match to sample__

__BASELINE: She is able to place single pieces of puzzles into their corresponding spaces when prompted\.__

Transfer at least 10 items from one container to another at every attempt\.

__*Visual perception is the brain’s ability to receive, interpret and act upon visual stimuli\. It involves components such as visual discrimination, memory, spatial relationships, visual form constancy, visual sequential memory, visual figures/ ground and visual closure\.*__

Place rings in a cone or stack at least 5 blocks at every given opportunity\.

Child was exposed to different puzzles\. She is able to start and complete single shapes inset puzzles\. She is able to sit through two different puzzles of similar forms\. Nevertheless, she is yet to independently complete three\-to\-four\-piece puzzles, requiring physical prompt to carry out\.

Her ability to match items continue to impress\. She was able to match identical objects when presented in a messy array of at most 3 items, usually requiring prompt to ensure compliance\. 

We also exposed her to sorting of colours and shapes for 2 different colours and shapes\. At the moment, Child is learning to sort two colours or two shapes for five items each\. We look forward to expanding this into more colours and shapes in the coming IP\.

She will match at least 25 identical objects or pictures in a messy array of at least 4

She will sort similar colors and shapes for 5 different colors or shapes\.

Complete inset puzzles of 3\-5 pieces\.

__Independent Play__

__BASELINE: She engages in self\-directed play__

Engage in cause\- and\- effect play, such as dumping things out of containers, pulling items out of cabinets, pushing buttons to makes sounds, Stacking and Knocking over blocks, pushing things, pulling toys, etc\. for at least 3 minutes using minimal prompt\.

__*This is when a child plays on his/her own without any involvement of an adult or caregiver\. It is targeted at improving the ability to manipulate and explore objects in no time\. Once s/he has attended to the object, then s/he is ready to explore it\.*__

Child demonstrated increasing functional use of toys and objects during independent play\. This includes actions such as cradling a doll, brushing hair with a brush and even feeding the doll with a plate or cup\. She has begun to manipulate and explore a variety of objects \(e\.g\., Lego blocks, play doh, inset puzzles, concrete objects\) to demonstrate an understanding of cause\-and\-effect relationships\. This includes actions like picking things out of containers and dumping things in containers, pulling items out of cabinets, and pushing/pulling things\. During outdoor play this IP, she was observed to demonstrate fascination for using the slide\.  

Impressively, Child has begun to demonstrate understanding of shifting attention between different presented toys during free play\. For instance, rather than focus solely on the first toy that catches her attention, she has increasingly demonstrated the need to move on to other toys at her disposal after extensively exploring one, when guided\. Specifically, Child also is able to make mild but consistent scribbles on paper using pencils and/or crayons for at least 3 minutes

She will engage in arts and crafts type activities such as coloring, drawing, painting, cutting, pasting, taping, stringing beads, play doh, etc\., for at least 5 minutes\.

She will spontaneously engage in pretend and imaginary play, \(sometimes with accompanying verbal behavior\), role\-playing, and acting out daily routines, movies, TV shows, or favorite activities at least 2 times in a three\-day period\.

__Social Behaviour and Social Play__

__BASELINE: She demonstrates ability to observe others in play\.  __

She will spontaneously follow or imitate motor behavior of an adult at least 5 times in a 4\-hour timed observation

*Social behaviour is a behaviour among two or more organisms or persons within the same species, and comprises any behaviour in which one member affects the other\. Social behaviour primarily involves interaction of the individual with their environment, as well as their response to same\. Social play, on the other hand is the collaborative interactions of peers during play\. These activities are geared towards achieving adjustments in inter individual cooperation*\.

Child’s social behaviour activities aimed to facilitate skills such as responding to others’ behaviours, sharing materials, turn taking, eye\-contact, etc\. Over the course of the IP, she required prompt and reinforcement to begin and sustain engagement\. Child was able to imitate actions of a peer leading a group music and movement task when provided moderate prompt\. On few occasions, she was also prompted to echo words initiated by an adult during group word play\. She demonstrated successful echoing on five recorded occasions\. She has however, not begun to respond to mands of peer requesting a motor behaviour from her\. Child’s ability to take turns and share toys with peers is emerging; we continue to encourage her participation and ensure compliance to instructions across different settings and people while providing appropriate reinforcement strategies\.  

She will take turns and share reinforcers with peers with prompts at least 3 times in a 4\- hour timed observation\.

She will spontaneously respond to the mands of peers 3 times in a single day\.

__Echoic__

__BASELINE: He makes spontaneous vocalizations and whole words __

She will echo at least 5 vowels,

diphthongs, or consonants on command

__Echoic measures a child’s ability to vocalise and imitate sounds, syllables, and to words\.__

Child’s echoic responses have not been consistent as she was only observed to echo words during five recorded observations\. In those instances, she correctly produced vowels e\.g\., /a/, /e/, /i/, and whole words e\.g\., apple, car, blue, etc\.

She will echo at least 5 whole words on command

__Mand__

__BASELINE__: She is able to make request by pulling an adult\.

Emit at least 2 different mands through use of Picture Exchange Communication System \(PECS\) for food, water and toy at least 2 times per day\.

__Mand is a measure of a child’s communication and language skills to request for items, actions or assistance based on motivation__\. 

When prompted with ‘what do you want?’, Child continues to learn how to mand for an item in sight by handing over a flashcard of the item to the adult\. She learnt to mand for items of high motivation to her such as food, water, and few toys\. Although, she has not begun to use words to make request, she had on two occasions requested for water through echoic responses after being modelled to\. 

Emit at least 2 different mands for reinforcers and/or food, water with verbal prompt every day through single word w/o intraverbal prompt \(I want…\)\.
